title: The Ladder of Desire — Ficino, Plato, and the Ascent of Eros
author: Plato / Marsilio Ficino

body.text: Ficino's De amore requires Plato's Symposium to make sense. Eros is the force that moves the soul from visible beauty toward the invisible order that gives beauty its power. The Ficinian path educates desire. It gives longing adirection.

He is neither mortal nor immortal, but in amean between them. He is agreat spirit, for the whole class of spiritual beings is intermediate between God and man, conveying and taking across to the gods the prayers of men, and to men the commands of the gods; and through them all divination passes, and the art of priests and sacrifices and purifications. God with man does not mingle, but the spiritual world is the mediator between them.

Love is intermediate. Adaimon — messenger between lack and fulfilment, moving between mortal and divine. This changes the status of desire for Ficino. The erotic force becomes the medium of ascent — amessenger, ago-between for mortal and divine.

body.caption: The daimonic intermediary — light moving between two worlds.

body.text: He who would proceed aright in this matter should begin from beautiful forms and mount upwards. He should love one fair form and then many, and learn the connection of them; and from beautiful bodies he should proceed to beautiful minds, and the beauty of laws and institutions, until he perceives that all beauty is of one kindred; and from institutions he should go on to the sciences, until at last the vision is revealed to him of asingle science of universal beauty.

The ladder begins at the first step. The beautiful body is incomplete, but real. The soul starts with one beloved form, then recognises beauty spreading through souls and laws and knowledge. Each step preserved and deepened. Ficino'serotic re-rendering: the world first appears as objects of desire. Then it becomes afield of signs. The beloved becomes transparent to ahigher beauty. Desire becomes contemplative.

body.caption: The ladder of love — ascent from visible beauty to the source.

body.text: He who has been instructed thus far in the things of love, and who has learned to see the beautiful in due order and succession, when he comes toward the end will suddenly perceive anature of wondrous beauty — beauty absolute, separate, simple, and everlasting, which without diminution and without increase is imparted to the ever-growing and perishing beauties of all other things.

At the summit, beauty appears as itself — absolute, without mixture. The soul discovers that every beautiful thing was apartial announcement. Ficino's Venus lives here: the cosmic power that draws the soul from multiplicity back to unity. When beauty strikes, let it educate the eye before the hand reaches.

Ficino'sspirituality gives longing avertical axis. The same force that binds the soul to surfaces can lift it through them. The lover becomes the philosopher. The beloved becomes the gate. Desire, educated, becomes the shape of return.
